data.body:
## Context
The DSO ticket system has grown to 445 tickets with 2,614 event files. The event-sourced reducer replays all events per-ticket, and ticket list invokes a separate Python process per ticket — at ~300ms each, 445 tickets takes ~17 seconds. Ticket commands are failing intermittently during sprint workflows. The system needs to handle a steady state of ~300 open tickets with peaks up to 1,000, across multiple concurrent environments (local dev machines, CI runners, worktrees). Three independent bottlenecks compound: (1) closed tickets that will never be modified still occupy the reducer's hot path, (2) event logs grow unbounded with comments and status changes, and (3) per-ticket subprocess invocation doesn't scale. The system already supports SNAPSHOT events (written by ticket-compact.sh, consumed by the reducer) and cross-environment sync via a split-phase fetch/merge/push protocol with flock serialization per machine.

## Success Criteria
1. ticket list completes in under 3 seconds at 300 open tickets and under 10 seconds at 1,000 open tickets, verified by a benchmark script that seeds N ticket directories, times the command, and exits non-zero when thresholds are breached — the batch reducer compiles all tickets in a single Python process invocation, replacing the current per-ticket subprocess pattern in the ticket list shell command
2. Closing a ticket via ticket transition automatically compacts that ticket's event log into a single SNAPSHOT event — the compaction file listing is moved inside the flock to prevent a race where concurrent writers add events between the file listing and lock acquisition
3. A ticket-lifecycle.sh script performs bulk compaction (tickets above 10-event threshold) and archival (ARCHIVED event marker on eligible tickets) in a single git commit, with a new 3-retry fetch-rebase-push loop for CI environments where local pushes may interleave
4. Archive eligibility uses transitive dependency traversal: starting from every open ticket, follow all depends_on/blocks edges recursively to find all reachable closed tickets; those reachable tickets are ineligible for archival. relates_to edges are ignored.
5. The reducer skips tickets with an ARCHIVED event when invoked by ticket list, but includes them when invoked by ticket show <id> — no directory movement, no merge conflict risk
6. A GitHub Actions workflow (ticket-lifecycle.yml) runs ticket-lifecycle.sh on a cron schedule with workflow_dispatch for manual triggering, using a concurrency group to bound parallelism (skipped runs are safe — compaction and archival are idempotent); after a successful run, locally synced tickets show compacted SNAPSHOTs for tickets that previously had more than 10 events

## Delivery Order
Stories are ordered so earlier deliverables unblock the immediate operational failures independently of later ones: (1) batch reducer, (2) compact-on-close with flock fix, (3) ARCHIVED event + reducer filtering, (4) lifecycle script + CI workflow. Each stage can be merged and validated independently.

## Dependencies
None

## Approach
Hybrid lifecycle: opportunistic compaction on ticket close (threshold overridden to 0, file-listing moved inside flock) + CI lifecycle job for bulk compact and archive. Archive implemented as an ARCHIVED event marker — the reducer filters on it, no directory moves needed. Batch reducer replaces per-ticket subprocess invocation in ticket list. Lifecycle job squashes all changes into a single git commit and uses a new fetch-rebase-retry loop for CI push safety. Transitive graph traversal for archive eligibility ensures closed tickets reachable from open tickets via depends_on/blocks chains are preserved. The lifecycle script accepts a configurable base path to operate in both the local worktree mount (.tickets-tracker/) and CI direct-checkout layouts.
